speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape five with Mrs. Rachel Warszauer Schlufman, August 29, 1995. You were telling me about the [NON-ENGLISH], so-called hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: While we-- while the police were carrying us on the stretchers, I was very embarrassed. I closed my eyes while I did see the people looking, you know, the ghetto people trying to look at our faces. But the blessing of my father, he put his hand on my forehead. And he says, God be with you. God will bless you. Don't worry, my child. God is every place. I heard his words, but I felt dizzy. I closed my eyes. I didn't want to face these people's faces. I was embarrassed. They took us to that [NON-ENGLISH], all three of us laying on these wooden beds with a little straw and a sheet would move back and forth. The straw was so rough. I felt so hot. I wanted water, water-- no water. It was forbidden to walk in, to walk in. But here and there, my mother tried to sneak in with some water to help us. When the policeman wasn't looking, you know, a Jewish policeman wasn't looking, she sneaked in. But he had the responsible job. He says if he's caught, that somebody will walk in and-- you know, and catch that disease and give to others. It was forbidden. He couldn't do it. He was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was always arguing with my mother, I assume, until my mother used to get in with just water to help us. And later, after a few days, she brought is hot tea. My aunt made it. And she cooked a little rice, you know, liquid rice she brought us to eat. She nourished us. But the second day while I was in that [NON-ENGLISH], a barber came in. And I say, are you a doctor? He says, no. He was wearing a white uniform, half a jacket, a sort of uniform. I say, what are you doing here? He says-- I thought maybe he's going to help me with medication. There was no medication.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says-- he looked at me. He was a Jewish barber. He didn't have the heart to say that my beautiful long braids are going to be cut off. He says, Miss. Warszauer, I want you to do me a favor. I was sent here. And I don't want to hurt you. But try to understand it's not my fault. I came to cut your hair off.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, what? Do you know what it means for a young girl to have their hair cut off? I says, but what are you-- how much are you going to cut off? He says, I'm going to shave your hair off. I says-- and he was holding the clipper. I says, shaving my hair off? I says, completely shaving my hair off? I started-- how sick I was, how my head was [INAUDIBLE], you know, hurting me and burning with temperature. I was screaming my head off. You're not going to cut my hair off.

speaker: interviewee
text: He held me with one hand, with his powerful hand. My-- my soft little hands, they hold me. But they were so weak. And with the other hand, he took the clipper. First, he cut off the braids. He took the clipper. And he shaved off my hair. Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't have any more tears. I didn't want to touch my head. I just felt that I couldn't sleep. And that straw was hurting me, because I didn't have hair. So my scalp was hurting me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was so ashamed. If I would have had guts, if I would have have-- if I would have a knife, if I would have, I would have killed myself. I didn't want to live anymore. That's it. What for?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-- my mother came in a few hours later. She was sniffing. She didn't cry anymore. She sniffed. And she hardly could say. She looked at me. And she says-- and I cried. And I says, mama, [SOBBING] I have no hair. She says, [NON-ENGLISH], my child. Don't cry. You are young. [NON-ENGLISH] It's only hair. Your hair will grow in in no time. You will see, my child. Your hair will be more beautiful than ever. You will have nicer hair later on. I looked at her. Hindi gave her a kerchief. She took out the kerchief. And she wrapped it around my head. And she put her cheeks to my face. She says, child, don't worry. You will have beautiful hair. And you will be beautiful again. And she stayed with me. He gave her about 10 minutes, and she left. Shmulek didn't say anything. Zisman says to me-- my cousin Zisman at the age of 10, he was such a wise little boy. And he says, I feel so sorry that I-- that I caused the whole thing and I brought the typhus to you and I brought you pain. I didn't answer him. And I turned on my side. And I tried to close my eyes to forget. Perhaps, I could sleep and forget about my pain. After two weeks, our home was open again. And we came back. My first look at my father when he came in, he loved me so dearly. He was so happy that I carried the name of his mother who died so young. He loved her so dearly, always talked about her. And I was so dear to my father, like every daughter is dear to their fathers. And he looked at me. And I stayed in bed with my naked head. He gave just one look. I did see his white face. And he walked over to the other room. And to-- to-- the first step by religious people, their faith, in their mind, is right away to take out the Talmud and learn to forget about their sorrows and about their pain. That lingers, their pain. With that, I'm watching my father taking out the Talmud and reading and didn't want to look at my head. But weeks and months passed by. My little hair started to grown in. And I stood in that little mirror that we had. And every day when I got up and I looked at how much it grew, how much it grew, I wanted the days to pass by so quickly that my little hair should grow in. In three months, it got about three inches, normally. And it was little curls around. My hair, you know, is a little curly naturally. And I was so-- I started to get so happy. I started to pick up my head. I started to walk in the ghetto street, you know, and not using anymore a kerchief with my hair short, short, short. But weeks and weeks until my hair grew in, and my mother says, you see, Rachela, I told you. You're going to have beautiful hair again. And that's-- I started to mellow down and live with the life what was waiting for me more, you know, for better days, for better life, you know, to expect while I was getting older. And in that time, a teenager was the most beautiful age, looking forward from my youth--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to improve.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin Zisman, after staying with us for weeks, my father started to teach him the bar mitzvah. He knew he hasn't got a father. He wanted-- but he didn't know nothing. But his mind was so occupied. His guilt was bothering him so much that the kid says, uncle, I have to go back. I left my mother with my little brother and sister in Warsaw ghetto. I have to see what they're doing. My-- I could never live. I have to go back and see their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father says, but how would you go? How would you go? He says, uncle, the way I came. That's the way I am going back. I will hustle and stay with the Poles on the farm until I will reach the ghetto. And I want to find my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war ended, I met a man coming from Israel. And he says-- and I met him in somebody else's house, in a friend's house. And we were talking. By coincidence, he tells me, is your name Warszauer? He says, I know a Warszauer in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: But meantime, I knew-- I heard that my cousin survived. Because after the war, my brother in England was searching for survivors. So my cousin knew maybe we survived. He was searching for the names. He heard we survived. We are in New York. He says, I know Warszauer, a Zisman Warszauer. He was a hussar. I say, but he's a hussar, too, my cousin. I says, [INAUDIBLE] when he told me he got married. But he, you know, after that, he gave me his address. And we started to write the correspondence to each other. His wish was the only wish. I should come to Israel and meet each other again. But life was so difficult in America then. I had two children and it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't want to get to America yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we'll go back to this later. I wanted you to tell me about the concentration camp in Skarzysko.

speaker: interviewee
text: About the concentration. Well, I would like to mention about my brother Zisman before. Before I went to Skarzysko, we had the ghetto life, like I-- like I told you, describe how life was. We had an ersatz synagogue. Somebody offered room to go in-- to come in and to say the prayer on Sabbath. My brother Zisman with my father used to attend that ersatz synagogue, we called it, to-- to-- to conduct the services on Saturday morning. But Zisman was working the whole week. He worked for the Germans. But on Saturday, he didn't work. But he worked on Sunday. So while he was davening-- he sang the prayer in that ersatz synagogue-- the Germans were running from room-- from house to house and taking out young men to work. They need it for certain work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom are they taking? They're coming into the ersatz synagogue, taking out two young men. My brother was among them. My brother was wearing that Sabbath suit. When he came to the committee, they took all these men-- there were about 20 of them-- to do hard work for them, taking to the Jewish committee. And from the Jewish committee, they counted them. And they marched in file to go for the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Zisman wasn't comfortable. He was wearing the Sabbath suit. He was frightened to be among these men. So he begged the Jewish policeman, do me a favor. It's only one block away. Let me go home. While you are busy counting and telling them and talking to them, I will just take off my suit and put on my working outfit, which I'm working everyday, and I feel comfortable. Because clothes were very expensive and very dear. He only had one suit and didn't want to ruin it. He didn't know what will happen in the end. Nobody can predict things, bad things. He meant it well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the policeman says, OK. But make sure you come back right away. So Zisman ran home. He took off his suit. He changed for his working suit, and he came back to committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he returned to committee, they all were gone. And Zisman was left alone. But his name was registered that he was gone. He gave it over to the Germans. Zisman came home. And he was telling my mother and father that they went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother with her premonition, she says, oy, [YIDDISH], you did the terrible thing. You should have gone with that suit. I fear, because they probably have your name. They're going to look after you. Sunday morning, two Jewish-- two Polish policemen came in with a warrant. Mr. Warszauer, your son is being addressed. We have to arrest him under the warrant under the Gestapo. He-- he didn't show up at the work. He ran away. You know, run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents pleaded with them to the police, try to understand. My son just came home to change his clothes. But he was running back. They were gone so fast. And when he returned, he felt very bad. But he didn't know where they were taking them. For the Germans, that's no excuse, the Polish policeman said. Mr. Warszauer, your son has to be arrested. They arrested my brother. They kept him for three days. After three days, the horse and wagon brought back my brother to the ghetto. I'll never forget all the inhabitants were, you know, ringing around, you know, stood around my brother. My father and mother and me-- perhaps my brother was there, too. But my mind, I couldn't see nobody. My eyes were filled with water, with tears. I watched my brother get up in that wagon and holding his hand up to Heaven. And he says, my dear people-- he talked like Moses-- don't be afraid. I tell you I will take revenge. My blood will take revenge. And with that, the wagon had left. My mother, she ran after that wagon. She holds her son's hand. She says, my son. She kissed his leg. She kissed his hand. She begged the Pole who was having my brother on that wagon not to go so fast. My mother, she pleaded with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said in my heart, looking at that horse, looking at that wagon, I felt like my heart was coming out. And I say, God, God in Heaven, is that horse stronger than we are, that he has a better chance to live than my brother? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in minutes, he was pulled away. My mother was laying on the ground and crying. She came home. She took out my brother's clothes for days and days and wrapped them around her face and cried that God should return her son. I couldn't take the pain anymore. I could not take the pain and the tears from my mother. I'm sitting outside on the same bench where I was sitting and waiting for my brother Berl. A postman-- which Jewish people did not receive no mail at that time. The mailman comes in to our-- to me and he says, Miss. Warszauer holding a postcard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he hands me a postcard. And I looked at that postcard. I says, but I tell him that, well, where does it come? Jewish people do not receive mail. He says, read it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm reading the postcard. And it says, if I will send 25 zlotys-- if the parents will send 25 zlotys to Auschwitz, they will send the ash-- the ashes from their son who was burned in that day. That was the beginning of Auschwitz when they started to experiment the people, when they sent away people, from, you know, priests, doctors, politicians to Auschwitz. That wasn't-- it wasn't a camp yet. That was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm reading that card. What should I do with that card? I ripped that card in pieces. And I threw it in the garbage.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cried for a few minutes. And I says, oh, God, my brother Zisman is gone. Berl is gone. Coming into the house, my mother still holds her Zisman's clothes in bags for her son, for God to have mercy that her son should survive and come back to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with that, it didn't take long. Just a couple of weeks, another surprise came out in our ghetto. What was the surprise? Zisman was taken away in 19-- almost the end of 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents never knew how their sons perished, how Berl perished, how Zisman perished. Only I knew in my heart. Weeks, months turned quickly. The year of 1942, OK, a new law arrived in our ghetto. That was in a hot July. I was the only girl left with my mother, with Moishe and with Shmulek. My mother, she-- she was young age of 49. I watched her, the way she was bent already, her eyes, her wrinkles. She changed so much from crying day in and day out. She still didn't believe. She only hoped the war would come to an end and everybody brought news. Perhaps the sons will survive. But I knew in my heart the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the rumors. Every girl from 16 to 18 who is going to register in 1942, will-- the city of Skarzysko will not be evacuated. The parents will have a chance to live. So my father came home from the committee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, they want me-- they wanted me to give you away to the camp. There's just about 18, 20 girls registered. But they need 50. They want to have 50 youngest and the best-- the best teenagers to go to the camp. I told him that my heart-- it's very hard to let a girl in that age to go away. But in a way, if you have a chance to survive, it would be good. And if you-- through you, we are going to survive, so you gain double deeds. So my child, I give you my word that God is everywhere in every corner. He's going to be with you. And I registered you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't say nothing. But I looked at my father, and I was shivering. I says, how will I [? manage ?] to go to a concentration-- to go to a camp? I didn't know what was there. I was mixed up. I couldn't sleep the whole night.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother didn't say nothing. She just looked at me. She just cried. She didn't look. She was, in a way, in a different world already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know which camp they went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Skarzysko camp was known as a big camp. They came from all over the cities, from Krakow, from Lodz, from-- from all Poland. From whole Poland, they brought the people to Skarzysko.

speaker: interviewer
text: But of course, we lived in ghetto-- I lived in ghetto with my parents. I didn't know what was doing there in the camp. I'm told it's a working camp. They will give me food, and I will work. How bad could it be? I couldn't imagine. Nobody came out from the camp and told us how bad it was there. So I says-- ah, my mother says, you will make friends. But do me a favor, she says. Just be decent as a young girl. You're going to be surrounded with men and women. Remember who you are. Remember who your mother and father are and-- and stay decent. And besides, keep yourself clean as a girl should. How far away was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about four miles away, walking distance. And that was an early morning. My mother went with me. When she says she accompanied me. She went with me. She wanted to give me her last steps, her last walk with me. She went with me while I walked to that place. There were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls went?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were about nearly 50 girls, like 40. I think they were saying about 20 girls were there already. And I knew them all. Everyone sat with sad faces, no mothers, no family, nothing. Because the SS stood with his rifle. Nobody could talk to these girls, come near. But my mother was holding me under her arm. I had a-- a little bundle and the bundle was-- was-- you know, just a little blanket, a towel, piece-- a piece of soap. I don't know from where she got it, probably from Aunt Hindi. And a comb she gave me. And she gave-- and I had an extra dress. That's all that I had in the ghetto. One dress, I washed it. The other one, I was wearing, you know, back and forth. And that extra dress, I had it with me. And that was the bundle that I carried with me. And that's all that I possessed. When we came, the SS man was standing close to the 20 girls. And he's seeing my mother walking with me. And he says, [GERMAN]. And I say, it's my mother, ma. And my mother says, OK, Rachela. He didn't let my mother give me a kiss. He gave her a pull away. She almost fell on the ground. I held my mouth. I thought he's going to take the rifle and shoot her. She walked away, but she was so bent down, you know, her head. It put a feeling in my heart that she's crying without a stop, leaving me behind. I looked with my eyes. I looked so far away in a shadow, looking after my mother how far I could see until she turned in that street. No more-- that was the last look [SOB] I saw my mother. Now, the concentration camp, another thing-- very, very tough to describe the pain and the misery which I witnessed in that camp. They took us-- we marched for the four-- for the four miles marching in that hot weather, seeing, looking at the Polish people. And I envied them so much. They were free to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Me, where are they going to take us? I did see after walking and walking on that hot day a sign, [GERMAN]. The gates opened for us. The SS men with their rifles-- the-- the-- the Lithuanians-- the Lithuanians were worse than the SS. You know? They were wearing dark uniforms with their rifles. But their smiles on their faces-- Jewish girls, proud of the beautiful Jewish girls, young, 16 to 18, perhaps 19. The oldest are 20-- ha, the cream from the-- from the Jewish people, yes? The beautiful daughters, they're marching into that camp. How long will they last, they thought to themselves. How long would they last? They didn't last long.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the lucky one and a few others who survived Skarzysko camp, I can tell you. We're always talking about Auschwitz. And Auschwitz, we all know in our heart how it was. But Skarzysko-Kamienna it was like an Auschwitz. There were many thousands and thousands and thousands of people were killed. First of all, Skarzysko camp was divided in three sections, work [INAUDIBLE], work, [INAUDIBLE]-- I was the lucky one. Work [INAUDIBLE] wasn't bad. Work B, 'Bee'-- we say it in English 'Bee,' B. C was the worst. Whoever was in camp C had no hope, had no possibility to survive. If a handful of people survived Work C, they were the luckiest. It was just a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop again.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
